title: I built a FIX protocol engine in Rust that's 4.5x faster than QuickFIX/J over TCP
author: Potential-Access-595
selftext:
Been working on this for a while — a FIX protocol engine for electronic trading. FIX is the standard protocol that banks and exchanges use to send orders and execution reports. Most shops run QuickFIX/J (Java) or one of the expensive commercial C++ engines.



I wanted to see how far Rust could push it. Zero-copy parsing, zero-alloc serialization, the whole deal.



Real benchmarks (not estimates):



28 ns heartbeat serialization

15.6 µs median TCP round-trip (NOS → ExecutionReport on localhost)

52.9 µs p99 vs QuickFIX/J's 342.7 µs (6.5x)

Max latency 125 µs vs QFJ's 3.9 ms — no GC pauses

111K msg/s sustained over TCP vs QFJ's 25K



The serializer is the fun one — 32 ns for a NewOrderSingle vs 917 ns in QuickFIX/J. Turns out writing directly into a pre-allocated byte buffer with lookup tables is fast.



It has the full session lifecycle — Logon, heartbeats, sequence tracking, gap detection, Logout. Both initiator and acceptor modes with a FixServer that auto-accepts connections with CompID whitelisting and a FixClient that's basically one function call:



let client = FixClient::new(FixClientConfig {

remote\_host: "10.0.1.50".into(),

remote\_port: 9878,

sender\_comp\_id: "BANK\_OMS".into(),

target\_comp\_id: "NYSE".into(),

..Default::default()

});

client.connect\_and\_run(&mut my\_app)?;



Also has SIMD delimiter scanning (NEON + SSE2), memory-mapped journaling, a Raft cluster module, Prometheus metrics, hardware timestamps for MiFID II, and an XML dictionary compiler. 238 tests including property-based fuzzing.



The QuickFIX/J comparison uses real TCP with proper session management on both sides — not just parsing byte arrays in a loop. Both benchmarks are in the repo so you can run them yourself.



GitHub: [https://github.com/matthart1983/velocitas-fix-engine](https://github.com/matthart1983/velocitas-fix-engine)



Would love feedback, especially from anyone who's worked with FIX engines in production. The transport layer is still pretty basic (blocking I/O, thread-per-connection) — considering mio or io\_uring for the next iteration.

comments.author: strat-run
comments.body:
Confirms my belief most firms probably have faster FIX implementations or have gone to native binary protocols.

Do you attribute the change to using Rust or just making better implementation choices?

replies.author: Potential-Access-595
replies.body: its a bit of both, Rust is inherently zero gc however an optimised Java implementation will also be performant

comments.author: ZealousidealShoe7998
comments.body: will look into it. i created a rust engine for trading with my broker with websocket but im getting 20ms rtt eventho my ping to the broker is 0.4ms so i might change to a broker with fix.
